threat_model.name: IndustrialSecure IoT/OT Platform — STRIDE Threat Model
threat_model.methodology: STRIDE + MITRE ATT&CK for ICS (ICS-ATT&CK v14) + Purdue Model Reference Architecture
threat_model.scope: End-to-end IndustrialSecure IoT/OT Platform: Level-0 field devices through Level-5 cloud. Covers all 35 components and 6 data flows across the Purdue Model zones.
assumptions: Threat actors include nation-state APT (Volt Typhoon, SANDWORM tier), criminal ransomware operators, and insider threats
Physical access to Level-0/1 wiring closets is assumed possible for motivated adversaries with insider or contractor access
IT network at Level-4 is considered semi-trusted (corporate laptop may be compromised)
Cloud (Level-5) is considered a separate trust domain; no implicit trust between cloud and on-prem OT
SIS (C003) physical isolation is assumed intact — threats modelled against data feed path only

threats.description: Attacker who has gained a foothold on the Level-2 SCADA network (via compromised engineering workstation or supply-chain implant) spoofs Modbus TCP/IP packets addressed to the PLC Data Collector on port 502. Forged READ_HOLDING_REGISTERS responses inject false sensor values (e.g., pressure sensor reading 12 PSI instead of actual 87 PSI) into the OPC-UA Gateway. The SCADA HMI then displays and acts on the falsified process state, potentially suppressing safety alarms or triggering incorrect actuator commands. Modbus TCP/IP has no authentication or message integrity — any node on the OT LAN that can reach port 502 can forge valid Modbus frames.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires prior foothold inside Level-2 OT LAN. Modbus has zero authentication. SANDWORM has demonstrated this exact capability (INDUSTROYER2, 2022). Moderate likelihood given growing supply-chain compromise vectors.
threats.impact_justification: False sensor readings can cause physical process deviation — overpressure, thermal runaway, or incorrect chemical mix ratio. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT. Could trigger or mask safety-critical events requiring SIS intervention.
mitigations: Deploy Modbus TCP/IP application firewall (e.g., Tosibox or Claroty virtual patch) to enforce read-only function codes from HMI direction
Implement Moxa MXview network topology map with anomaly alerting on unexpected Modbus source IPs
Enable Claroty xDome (C029) protocol anomaly detection — alert on Modbus function codes never seen in baseline (FC6/FC16 writes from non-authorised IP)
Conduct Modbus register map inventory and whitelist expected register value ranges; alert on statistical outliers
Migrate PLC interface to OPC-UA with certificate-based session authentication at next planned maintenance window (IEC 62443 SL-2 requirement)
Implement unidirectional data diode for read-only sensor feeds toward Level-3 where write-back is not required

threats.description: Kepware KEPServerEX exposes OPC-UA endpoint on port 4840. Anonymous authentication mode, if enabled (a default in older Kepware versions), allows any client on the OT LAN to establish an OPC-UA session without a certificate. An attacker who has lateral-moved into the Level-2 SCADA network can connect as an anonymous OPC-UA client and, if write-capable tags are exposed (e.g., setpoint registers), issue NodeId write calls to modify PLC setpoints directly — bypassing operator HMI controls and SCADA interlocks. This constitutes elevation from network-level access to physical process control authority.
threats.likelihood_justification: Anonymous OPC-UA access is enabled by default in Kepware prior to v6.11. Even with certificate auth configured, self-signed cert acceptance policies weaken the control. Exploitation requires only network access to port 4840 and publicly documented OPC-UA SDK.
threats.impact_justification: Direct write access to PLC setpoints allows an attacker to command actuators to dangerous states. Turbine speed setpoints, valve positions, heater temperatures — all writable. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT. Equivalent to operator-level physical control of the plant.
mitigations: Immediately audit Kepware OPC-UA server configuration: disable Anonymous security mode, enforce SignAndEncrypt (Basic256Sha256)
Enforce x509 certificate authentication for all OPC-UA client sessions; reject self-signed certificates
Restrict write-capable OPC-UA tags to only HMI service accounts; all data export paths to Level-3 must use read-only OPC-UA subscriptions
Implement OPC-UA firewall at Level-2 DMZ allowing only explicit NodeId access by authorised client certificates
Upgrade Kepware to KEPServerEX v6.14 (current) and apply all OPC Foundation security patches
Log all OPC-UA session establishment events to Claroty IDS; alert on sessions from unexpected certificate thumbprints

threats.description: An attacker with access to the SCADA HMI (e.g., via compromised Windows session through a VPN-tunnelled RDP from an IT laptop, or via a USB-dropped malware on the HMI workstation) manipulates process control parameters directly in Wonderware SCADA. This includes modifying alarm setpoints (raising threshold so genuine anomalies go undetected), altering PID controller tuning parameters to destabilise a control loop, or issuing direct actuator commands outside normal operating ranges. Because SCADA operators are trusting the HMI display, manipulated setpoints may not be noticed until a physical process deviation occurs.
threats.likelihood_justification: SCADA HMI is on a dedicated air-gapped VLAN per the architecture. However, HMI compromise via removable media (USB) or supply-chain software update is a known vector (Stuxnet, TRITON). CIS Level-2 hardening provides strong mitigation.
threats.impact_justification: Manipulation of SCADA HMI control parameters can directly cause uncontrolled process conditions. Alarm suppression specifically is a TRITON-class technique designed to prevent SIS activation. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT.
mitigations: Implement application whitelisting (Windows AppLocker / Carbon Black) on all SCADA HMI workstations — only authorised Wonderware binaries may execute
Disable all USB ports on HMI workstations via Group Policy and physical port blockers
Enable Wonderware SCADA audit log for all setpoint changes, alarm acknowledgements, and operator commands — forward to Splunk SIEM
Require dual-operator confirmation for any setpoint change beyond ±10% of nominal (two-person integrity rule)
Patch SCADA OS via offline WSUS server (air-gapped) — no direct internet patching permitted
Deploy Secomea secure remote access appliance for any emergency remote HMI access, with full session recording

threats.ics_attack_tactic: Inhibit Response Function → Impair Process Control
threats.description: An attacker who has compromised a single edge device (e.g., via firmware vulnerability or physical tamper) can use that device's valid mTLS certificate to publish crafted MQTT messages on the SCADA display topic (site/plant/device/<id>/telemetry). Because the MQTT broker's ACL is per-device-certificate and topic-based, a compromised device can only publish to its own topic namespace — however, if topic ACLs are overly permissive (e.g., wildcard site/# grants), the compromised device can poison telemetry for ALL devices on the site, poisoning SCADA HMI displays and Edge ML inference with false sensor values. Additionally, a broker-level resource exhaustion attack (crafting 10,000 concurrent MQTT connections from the compromised device) can deny all other devices from connecting, causing telemetry blackout.
threats.likelihood_justification: Mosquitto 2.0 has ACL support but misconfigurations are common in industrial deployments. Compromise of a single edge device is a realistic threat given diverse IoT device firmware quality. The attack requires only one compromised device certificate.
threats.impact_justification: SCADA display poisoning via MQTT causes operators to make incorrect control decisions based on false data. Telemetry blackout prevents anomaly detection. High impact but SIS provides an independent safety backstop for the most critical conditions.
mitigations: Enforce strict per-device ACLs: each device certificate may ONLY publish to site/<site_id>/device/<device_id>/# — no wildcard topic grants
Enable Mosquitto connection rate limiting (max_connections_per_second 50) and per-client connection limits to prevent resource exhaustion
Implement MQTT message size limits (max_packet_size 256KB) and QoS limits to prevent broker overload
Monitor for unexpected MQTT publisher device IDs using Claroty xDome OT IDS — alert on first-seen device publishing to new topic
Rotate device certificates quarterly; Certificate Authority (C020) enforces certificate pinning on broker side
Implement Mosquitto ACL audit log forwarded to Splunk SIEM — alert on ACL denials and unexpected topic patterns

threats.description: A man-in-the-middle attacker positioned between the MinIO object store and the Firmware Update Service intercepts the OTA firmware delivery to edge devices. If the HTTPS session to MinIO is terminated at an intermediate proxy (e.g., a transparent SSL inspection appliance), the attacker can substitute a malicious firmware image in place of the legitimate signed artifact. Even with Ed25519 signature verification on the device, if the private signing key stored in HashiCorp Vault (C021) is compromised, the attacker can sign arbitrary firmware. Additionally, if the MinIO S3-compat endpoint has a public network interface or misconfigured IAM policy, an attacker can directly overwrite firmware objects before they are fetched by the update service.
threats.likelihood_justification: Ed25519 signing provides strong protection if key management is sound. The primary risk is Vault compromise (C021) or MinIO ACL misconfiguration. HSM-backed Vault keys significantly reduce this risk. Classified as a medium-sophistication nation-state technique.
threats.impact_justification: Malicious firmware deployed to edge gateways or field devices provides persistent attacker access to the OT network. Firmware-level implant is extremely difficult to detect or remove. Constitutes full supply-chain compromise of the OT fleet. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT if firmware manipulates sensor reporting or actuator commands.
mitigations: Enforce TLS certificate pinning on Firmware Update Service → MinIO connection — reject any HTTPS session without MinIO's specific server certificate
Enable MinIO WORM Object Lock on the firmware bucket — firmware objects immutable after write; requires privileged credentials to overwrite
Audit HashiCorp Vault PKI secrets engine: restrict Ed25519 signing key access to the Firmware Update Service AppRole only, with audit logging on every signing operation
Implement firmware manifest file (separate from the binary) containing expected SHA-256 hash, device model, and version — validate before signature check
Enable staged rollout monitoring: canary (1%) must report successful boot and expected telemetry before pilot expansion; automatically halt rollout on canary failure
Implement firmware transparency log (e.g., Sigstore/Rekor) so all published firmware versions are publicly auditable — detect rogue signing events
HSM-backed Vault auto-unseal (AWS KMS CMK) ensures Vault master key is never exposed in software

threats.description: Kafka is the single ingest backbone for all 50,000 sensor data points per second. An attacker with access to the Level-4 network (e.g., via a compromised Kubernetes service account or lateral movement from a developer workstation) can launch a Kafka partition leader exhaustion attack: by producing millions of tiny messages to a single partition, they cause the KRaft controller to stall during partition rebalance, triggering a rolling broker restart. Alternatively, a malicious Kafka consumer that joins and immediately departs a consumer group in a loop forces continuous group rebalances, blocking all legitimate consumers for tens of seconds on each cycle. During Kafka outage, all real-time telemetry from field devices is lost. SCADA operators lose visibility into plant conditions. Anomaly detection stops. Alert Processing Service cannot generate alarms. This creates a 'telemetry blindness' window that may be exploited as a preparation step before a physical attack.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires Level-4 network access or compromised Kafka client credentials. SASL/SCRAM authentication limits access, but a compromised microservice pod could abuse its consumer group membership. Kafka DoS is well-documented in CVE history.
threats.impact_justification: Loss of telemetry visibility (Denial of View) at industrial scale can mask physical process deviations. SIS (C003) operates independently but operator situational awareness is severely degraded. Classified HIGH due to safety-adjacent impact.
mitigations: Enable Kafka quotas (producer-byte-rate and consumer-fetch-max-bytes) per SASL principal — prevent any single client from monopolising broker bandwidth
Implement Kafka topic-level ACLs: each microservice SASL credential has write access to only its producing topics and read access to only its consuming topics
Configure min.insync.replicas=2 and acks=all on all critical telemetry topics to ensure no data loss even during single-broker restart
Deploy Kafka UI (Confluent Control Center or Kafka Manager) with alert on consumer group lag > 60s for raw-telemetry topic — page on-call NOC
Isolate Kafka brokers in a dedicated Kubernetes namespace with Cilium network policy: only authorised service accounts may connect to port 9092
Implement circuit breaker in Alert Processing Service (C017): if Kafka lag exceeds threshold, activate local in-memory alert buffer and page SOC independently of Kafka availability

threats.description: The Palo Alto PA-3440 NGFW at the Level-3.5 Industrial DMZ is the primary enforcement point preventing IT-to-OT lateral movement. A PAN-OS vulnerability (heap buffer overflow in the management interface, as seen in CVE-2024-3400) exploited from an IT-side attacker allows root-level code execution on the firewall itself. With firewall OS control, the attacker can modify the security policy to open a bidirectional flow from Level-4 to Level-2, effectively collapsing the Purdue Model DMZ. This grants IT-network attackers unrestricted access to SCADA and PLC components without any further exploitation. This is the classic IT-to-OT pivot path documented in NERC-CIP incident reports.
threats.likelihood_justification: PAN-OS management interface vulnerabilities are actively exploited by nation-state actors (e.g., UTA0218 exploiting CVE-2024-3400 in 2024). The management interface must be patched promptly. The firewall is a high-value target for any actor seeking OT access.
threats.impact_justification: Firewall compromise collapses the primary OT/IT segmentation control. All IEC 62443 Zone boundaries are rendered ineffective. Attacker gains direct access to Level-2 SCADA and Level-1 PLCs. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT through cascading OT access.
threats.iec62443_zone: DMZ — Conduit between OT and IT
mitigations: Restrict Palo Alto management interface to dedicated out-of-band management VLAN — no management access from production OT or IT subnets
Apply PAN-OS patches on emergency schedule (24h SLA for CVSS 9.0+) using offline update package from Palo Alto support portal
Enable Palo Alto Threat Prevention and DNS Security subscriptions; subscribe to Palo Alto Unit 42 threat feeds for ICS-targeted IOCs
Implement configuration change control: all PAN-OS policy changes require change management ticket and dual-admin approval via Panorama
Deploy secondary (standby) NGFW for HA: active-passive pair ensures attack on primary does not eliminate enforcement entirely
Forward all firewall logs to Splunk SIEM with correlation rule: alert on any new flow from Level-4 to Level-2 or lower (should be zero)

threats.description: The NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX edge gateway (C008) runs Ubuntu 22.04 with K3s Kubernetes and holds in-memory: WireGuard private keys, mTLS client certificates (for MQTT and cloud connections), and buffered telemetry from hundreds of OT devices. A supply-chain compromised container image deployed via K3s, or a Linux kernel exploit (e.g., dirty pipe successor), allows an attacker to dump the edge gateway memory and exfiltrate all active keying material. With the WireGuard private key and device certificates, the attacker can establish an authenticated WireGuard tunnel to the AWS VPC (C011) and a valid MQTT mTLS connection to the broker, enabling persistent covert access to the cloud telemetry stream and command injection into the OTA pipeline.
threats.likelihood_justification: Edge gateways are physically accessible at remote plant sites where physical security may be weaker. K3s container supply-chain risks are real. dm-verity provides strong root-of-trust but relies on secure boot configuration being intact.
threats.impact_justification: WireGuard key and certificate exfiltration provides authenticated access to cloud (Level-5) and MQTT (Level-3) from any internet location. Breaks the cloud trust boundary. Enables persistent, authenticated attacker presence without raising alarms.
mitigations: Enable UEFI Secure Boot on Jetson Orin NX; configure dm-verity to cover all system partitions — attacker cannot persist without breaking measured boot
Store WireGuard private keys and mTLS certificates in a TPM 2.0 chip (Jetson supports Discrete TPM via SPI); keys never exposed to software, only used via TPM crypto operations
Implement container image signing (Notary v2 / cosign) and K3s admission webhook that rejects unsigned or unverified images
Enable Falco runtime security on the K3s cluster: alert on process attempting to read /proc/*/mem, /dev/mem, or execute ptrace outside kubectl exec sessions
Rotate WireGuard pre-shared keys every 30 days via automated step-ca ACME renewal; device certificates have 90-day expiry — compromise window is bounded
Implement physical tamper detection on edge gateway enclosure (e.g., Sealey Security seal with QR code); inspect before each maintenance window

threats.description: Physical-layer attack: an adversary with physical access to plant-floor wiring (Level-0) tampers with the 4-20mA analog loop or Modbus RTU RS-485 bus. For a 4-20mA pressure sensor, injecting a resistor or current source into the loop generates a false 20mA reading (indicating 100% of range) regardless of actual process pressure. For Modbus RTU, a rogue device attached to the RS-485 bus can win the master-slave arbitration (Modbus RTU has no collision detection) and respond with forged register values before the legitimate slave device responds. The PLC Data Collector (C002) receives the spoofed value, propagates it to OPC-UA Gateway and SCADA HMI. Operators see safe values while the physical process may be in an unsafe state.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires Level-0 physical access, which is controlled by badge+biometric per architecture. However, contractor access, insider threat, or physical perimeter breach could enable this. This is a known nation-state technique for sabotage operations.
threats.impact_justification: Masked sensor readings prevent operators and control systems from detecting dangerous process conditions. A pressure sensor showing safe values while actual pressure is critical could delay emergency shutdown trigger. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT — potential injury, explosion, or environmental release.
mitigations: Implement field cable tamper detection: fiber-optic cable sheathing with optical continuity monitoring on critical sensor runs
Deploy independent SIS (C003) with hardwired I/O that reads raw physical signals independently of the Modbus data path — SIS cannot be deceived by Modbus spoofing
Cross-validate SCADA-displayed sensor values against historian trend rates-of-change — a sensor reading jumping from 45 PSI to 100 PSI instantaneously triggers alarm
Install surveillance cameras with motion detection covering all Level-0 field device junction boxes and marshalling cabinets
Require escort and sign-in log for all contractor access to Level-0 areas; maintain visitor log in PI Historian (C007) for audit
Conduct annual physical security assessment of field wiring runs (CIP-006 compliance)

threats.description: The SCADA Interface Layer (C006) is a C++17/Python 3.12 bridge that converts OPC-UA typed data to Protobuf messages. As a mixed-language component, it has both native (C++) crash surfaces and Python exception paths. An attacker who has gained shell access to this component can modify the Python wrapper code in-place (no dm-verity on the Python layer) to drop or alter specific sensor readings before they are published to MQTT — selectively suppressing alarms for target process variables while leaving others intact to avoid obvious telemetry blackout. Because the SCADA Interface Layer does not cryptographically sign its output, there is no way to prove post-hoc that published MQTT values matched actual OPC-UA subscriptions. Operators cannot prove whether the process was running safely during the manipulation window.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires code execution inside the SCADA Interface Layer container/process. Possible via Python deserialization exploit (Protobuf parsing of malformed upstream input) or supply-chain compromise of Python dependencies. Complex attack chain but medium-sophistication actor could achieve it.
threats.impact_justification: Selective suppression of process alarms is the most dangerous manipulation — it creates a false sense of normalcy while a physical safety condition develops. Loss of non-repudiation means post-incident forensics cannot reconstruct the true timeline of process values.
mitigations: Implement HMAC-SHA256 message authentication on all MQTT messages published by SCADA Interface Layer using a device-specific key from Vault — receiver can verify integrity
Freeze Python runtime: use PyInstaller or Nuitka to compile Python wrapper to native binary; deploy with dm-verity in the container image layer
Enable container image pinning via SHA256 digest in K3s pod spec — any modification to the runtime image is detected at pod restart
Implement dual-source telemetry validation: compare OPC-UA historian values (C007) against MQTT stream values with 5-minute lag; alert on divergence > 2% for critical tags
Forward all OPC-UA subscription events from Kepware to Splunk SIEM as an independent audit trail — compare against MQTT-published values nightly
Implement SAST (Semgrep) in CI/CD pipeline for C++ and Python components: scan for buffer overflows, Protobuf parsing vulnerabilities, and unsafe deserialization

threats.description: The step-ca Certificate Authority (C020) is the root of trust for all device mTLS, MQTT client certificates, and OPC-UA session certificates across the entire OT platform. Compromise of the step-ca signing key (stored in Vault's PKCS#11 HSM backend) allows an attacker to issue arbitrary certificates appearing valid to all platform components. A rogue device certificate allows the attacker to: authenticate to the MQTT broker as a legitimate device, establish valid OPC-UA sessions to Kepware, and authenticate to the WireGuard VPN. The step-ca ACME v2 and EST (RFC 7030) endpoints, if exposed beyond their intended scope, may accept certificate signing requests from unauthorized clients by exploiting ACME account spoofing or EST TLS bypass vulnerabilities.
threats.likelihood_justification: Vault HSM backend significantly raises the bar for CA key compromise. The primary risk is ACME/EST endpoint exposure and authorization logic bugs in step-ca provisioner configuration. step-ca 0.25.2 has a well-tested code path but ACME provisioner configuration errors are common in industrial deployments.
threats.impact_justification: CA compromise is a platform-level trust failure. Every certificate-authenticated component (MQTT, OPC-UA, WireGuard, service mesh) becomes untrustworthy. Requires full certificate revocation and reissuance across the entire device fleet — weeks of remediation. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT through downstream OT component access.
mitigations: Store step-ca root CA private key offline (hardware token, never network-connected); issue only subordinate intermediate CA from the online step-ca instance
Restrict step-ca ACME and EST endpoints to specific Kubernetes service account CIDR ranges via network policy — no external access
Audit step-ca provisioner configuration: each provisioner (ACME, JWK, Kubernetes OIDC) must have restrictive claims (allowed-names regex, max-cert-duration)
Enable step-ca audit log (webhook or file) forwarded to Splunk SIEM — alert on unexpected certificate subject names, especially OU=device with unusual device IDs
Implement Certificate Transparency log (internal) for all issued device certificates; run regular audit comparing CT log against device registry in C018
Test CA key compromise response plan annually: simulate CA compromise, measure time to issue CRL/OCSP revocation and re-enroll full device fleet

threats.description: The OSIsoft PI System historian (C007) stores 10 years of compressed process time-series at 1-second resolution. This operational data constitutes a detailed blueprint of the plant's normal operating envelope: temperature ranges, pressure cycles, flow rate signatures, batch timing, and throughput capacity. An attacker who gains access to the PI Web API endpoint (exposed over HTTPS port 443 to the Time-Series Ingestion Service) can exfiltrate the full historical dataset. This operational intelligence is highly valuable for: (1) reverse-engineering the physical process for sabotage planning, (2) understanding the production capacity for competitive intelligence, (3) identifying the specific timing windows when process deviations would be hardest to detect (e.g., shift changes, maintenance windows). OSIsoft PI has a history of unauthenticated API access CVEs.
threats.likelihood_justification: PI Web API is accessible to Level-4 services. OSIsoft PI vulnerabilities including unauthenticated data access have been disclosed and are actively targeted. Any compromised Level-4 microservice can access the PI Web API endpoint without additional exploitation.
threats.impact_justification: Process data exfiltration is a pre-attack reconnaissance activity. Primary impact is strategic intelligence loss and regulatory violation (NERC-CIP-011). No immediate physical safety impact, but high consequence for competitive and national security contexts.
mitigations: Restrict PI Web API to a service account used exclusively by C016 (Time-Series Ingestion Service); implement PI AF role-based access control with per-attribute-level read permissions
Enable PI Server audit log for all bulk data export operations (> 10,000 points in single query) — forward to Splunk SIEM
Implement PI Web API query rate limiting: maximum 1,000 points per minute per authenticated principal; bulk export requires change management approval
Apply OSIsoft PI security patches on monthly schedule; subscribe to OSIsoft security advisories (now AVEVA — Schneider Electric)
Encrypt PI archive files at rest using PI Server built-in encryption (FIPS 140-2 compliant cipher)
Implement Data Loss Prevention: network flow monitoring to detect > 50 MB PI Web API response within a 5-minute window

threats.description: The WireGuard tunnel (C011) is the only permitted path from Level-3 edge sites to the AWS cloud (Level-5). WireGuard uses a stateless handshake with no DoS amplification protection built into the protocol — a flood of crafted initiation packets to UDP port 51820 can consume the edge gateway CPU processing handshakes, starving legitimate tunnel traffic. An ISP-level or on-path attacker can also simply drop WireGuard UDP traffic, causing the tunnel to fail silently. Since the architecture relies on this tunnel for cloud telemetry forwarding (C031), model updates (C033), and GuardDuty IoT detection (C035), tunnel failure isolates the cloud monitoring stack from the OT site — operators lose the cloud-analytics view and any cloud-triggered alerting.
threats.likelihood_justification: WireGuard UDP 51820 is easily identified by network-level observers. ISP-level BGP hijacking has been used to selectively disrupt specific UDP flows. The Jetson Orin NX has limited CPU to spare for high-rate handshake processing.
threats.impact_justification: Loss of cloud connectivity reduces monitoring capability but on-prem Level-4 services continue operating. SCADA and PLCs are unaffected. SIS operates independently. Classified MEDIUM — degraded visibility, not loss of control.
threats.iec62443_zone: DMZ — Conduit between OT and IT
mitigations: Implement WireGuard packet rate limiting (iptables -m hashlimit on UDP 51820) on the edge gateway to throttle handshake initiation rate per source IP
Configure WireGuard persistent keepalives (25s) so tunnel failure is detected promptly; implement automatic tunnel restart via systemd unit with ExecStartPre connectivity check
Provision a secondary out-of-band LTE/5G cellular modem on the edge gateway for backup cloud connectivity when primary WAN is disrupted
Implement local alarm buffering in Alert Processing Service (C017): all severity-3+ alerts queued locally and replayed when WireGuard tunnel recovers
Monitor WireGuard tunnel statistics (handshake last-seen, bytes-received) via Prometheus node_exporter WireGuard plugin; alert if no handshake in 5 minutes

threats.description: The Alert Processing Service (C017) is the primary path by which OT anomalies reach human operators and the SIEM. An attacker who has compromised a Kubernetes pod in the same namespace can exploit Celery task queue vulnerabilities to inject task messages that silently drop or acknowledge specific alert patterns without routing them to PagerDuty or Splunk. Because Redis is the Celery task backend, an attacker with Redis access (via a compromised service account) can purge queued alert tasks before they are processed. Post-incident, there is no cryptographic proof that specific Kafka alert messages were consumed and acted upon — only Splunk HEC receipt timestamps, which can be gapped. In a NERC-CIP CIP-008 incident investigation, missing alert records constitute compliance violation evidence.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires compromise of the Kubernetes namespace hosting Alert Processing Service or Redis. Cilium network policy limits cross-namespace access, but a compromised pod in the same namespace has full Redis access via service DNS. Celery task queue injection requires knowledge of the message format.
threats.impact_justification: Suppression of OT alerts during a physical attack is the key preparatory step before executing process manipulation. Operators who do not receive critical alerts cannot initiate emergency procedures. SAFETY-ADJACENT: alert suppression can delay SIS activation in non-hardwired pathways.
mitigations: Sign all Kafka alert messages with HMAC-SHA256 using a key stored in Vault; Alert Processing Service verifies signature before processing — forged or replayed messages rejected
Implement Redis AUTH with a randomly-generated 64-byte password, rotated by Vault dynamic secrets engine (1h TTL) — limits window for stolen credential reuse
Deploy Kafka consumer offset monitoring: if alert consumer group has not committed an offset advance in 30 seconds, immediately page on-call NOC regardless of Redis/Celery state
Implement out-of-band alert forwarding: direct Syslog-over-TLS from Claroty xDome to Splunk SIEM (DF005) bypasses Kafka and Alert Processing Service entirely for OT IDS events
Retain raw Kafka messages in the alerts topic for 7 days (architecture specifies this); implement nightly reconciliation comparing Kafka topic offsets against Splunk HEC receipt counts
Isolate Redis to a dedicated pod with Kubernetes NetworkPolicy: only Alert Processing Service pods may connect on port 6379

threats.description: HashiCorp Vault 1.15.6 is the centralised secrets store holding database credentials, API keys, and the PKI root for the entire platform. Vault's Kubernetes auth method uses projected service account tokens — a compromised Kubernetes pod with any service account can attempt to authenticate to Vault AppRoles it was not assigned. If AppRole secret-IDs are stored as Kubernetes Secrets (rather than dynamically fetched by an init container), they are accessible to any pod that can read Kubernetes Secrets in the same namespace (or to cluster-admin). An attacker who achieves cluster-admin access (via RKE2 control-plane compromise or OPA Gatekeeper bypass) can retrieve all Vault tokens, obtaining the InfluxDB write token, TimescaleDB password, and firmware signing credentials — full platform compromise.
threats.likelihood_justification: Vault is well-hardened (PKCS#11 auto-unseal, AppRole + K8s auth). The primary risk vector is Kubernetes control-plane compromise enabling secret extraction. RKE2 CIS hardening significantly reduces this. However, a single misconfigured ClusterRoleBinding can expose secrets to all service accounts.
threats.impact_justification: Vault compromise provides access to ALL platform credentials simultaneously: DB write access, firmware signing key, CA key, device certificate provisioning. Enables full platform compromise including physical-process-level impact. PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT through cascading access.
mitigations: Never store Vault AppRole secret-IDs as Kubernetes Secrets — use Vault Agent Injector sidecar with response_wrap_ttl=60s to deliver wrapped secret-IDs directly to application pods
Implement Vault entity aliases with strict namespace isolation: each microservice's AppRole has ONLY the Vault policies required for its function (least privilege)
Enable Vault audit backend logging ALL requests to a dedicated audit log volume; forward to Splunk SIEM — alert on any AppRole login from unexpected Kubernetes namespace
Set Vault token max_ttl=1h for all dynamic secrets; 8h for service tokens (architecture specifies 8h service certs — align to this)
Implement Vault Sentinel policies (Vault Enterprise) or OPA-based policy enforcement to reject secret reads from pod service accounts outside expected namespaces
Conduct quarterly Vault token and AppRole audit: list all active tokens, verify each maps to a running service, revoke orphans

threats.description: AWS IoT Core device certificates, issued by step-ca (C020), authenticate edge devices to IoT Core topic namespaces. If an attacker exfiltrates an edge device's private key (e.g., via CVE-exploiting the K3s runtime on C008), they can register the same certificate with a rogue MQTT client connecting directly to the AWS IoT Core endpoint (bypassing the WireGuard tunnel entirely). AWS IoT Core will accept the connection as the legitimate device. The rogue client can then publish fabricated telemetry to AWS Timestream (C032) and device shadow state, corrupting the cloud analytics dataset used for SageMaker ML model training. Poisoned training data degrades anomaly detection model accuracy — subsequent retrained models deployed to edge gateways will have reduced sensitivity to real process anomalies.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires edge device private key exfiltration (see TM-IOT-008). IoT Core device policies should restrict each certificate to its own topic namespace, limiting blast radius. ML training data poisoning requires sustained access over multiple training cycles.
threats.impact_justification: Training data poisoning has a delayed, subtle impact on anomaly detection accuracy. Not an immediate safety threat but reduces long-term platform effectiveness. Direct telemetry spoofing in AWS is lower impact than OT-side spoofing since AWS data does not feedback to SCADA control.
mitigations: Enable AWS IoT Core device policies restricting each certificate to ONLY its assigned topic namespace (site/<site_id>/device/<device_id>/# allow; deny $aws/rules/# from field devices)

Implement IoT Core Just-in-Time Registration (JITR) with Lambda validation: each new certificate connection triggers a Lambda function that validates device fingerprint against device registry (C018) before accepting connection
Enable GuardDuty IoT Threat Detection: detect concurrent connections from same certificate ID from two different IP addresses (certificate cloning indicator)

Implement ML training data validation in SageMaker pipeline: detect statistical anomalies in training batch (e.g., sudden change in sensor value distribution that differs from historical baseline by > 3 sigma)
Rotate all IoT Core device certificates every 90 days via step-ca ACME automated renewal — limits credential reuse window

threats.description: RKE2 runs all Level-4 microservices including the firmware update pipeline, device management, and alert processing. An attacker who has compromised a single application pod (e.g., via a FastAPI deserialization vulnerability in C017, or a Go gRPC parsing bug in C016) can attempt to escape to the RKE2 control plane via misconfigured RBAC. If any service account has a ClusterRoleBinding granting broader permissions than intended (a common Helm chart default), the attacker can enumerate all secrets, exec into any pod, and escalate to cluster-admin. Cluster-admin access provides: full Vault token access (via K8s auth), all namespace secrets, ability to modify pod specs to exfiltrate data or plant backdoors in firmware pipeline containers.
threats.likelihood_justification: Container escape and K8s privilege escalation is a well-documented attack class. RKE2 CIS benchmark hardening and OPA Gatekeeper reduce the surface, but RBAC misconfigurations in Helm-deployed services are endemic across the industry. Cilium network policy does not prevent intra-namespace lateral movement.
threats.impact_justification: Cluster-admin escalation provides control over all Level-4 services including the firmware pipeline, device management, and secrets access. Enables supply-chain attack against edge device fleet. High impact; safety impact is indirect through firmware pipeline.
mitigations: Implement OPA Gatekeeper constraint: deny ClusterRoleBinding to any service account unless explicitly allowlisted — catch Helm chart defaults that grant cluster-admin
Enable Kubernetes audit logging at RequestResponse level; forward to Splunk SIEM — alert on any exec/attach events in production namespaces outside change window
Implement Falco runtime rules: alert on container escape indicators (nsenter, /proc/1/ns manipulation, mount of host filesystem)
Run regular automated RBAC audit using kubectl-who-can or rbac-police tools — fail CI pipeline if any service account has wildcards on sensitive verbs (get/list secrets)
Enforce Pod Security Standards (Restricted profile) cluster-wide via Admission Controller: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, no privilege escalation
Isolate the firmware update pipeline (C019) in a dedicated namespace with enhanced PodSecurityAdmission and dedicated node pool — compromise of other services cannot reach firmware signing

threats.description: The Claroty xDome passive IDS relies on a SPAN port tap on the industrial DMZ firewall (C012). If an attacker can configure the Palo Alto firewall to disable or redirect the SPAN port (via firewall management interface compromise, see TM-IOT-007), the Claroty sensor receives no traffic and all OT protocol anomaly detection goes dark. Additionally, a sophisticated attacker aware of Claroty's OT protocol parser behavior can craft Modbus and OPC-UA traffic that is technically valid (and thus not anomalous) but semantically executes a process deviation — exploiting the gap between protocol validity and semantic safety. Claroty detects known-bad patterns, not unknown-valid-but-dangerous command sequences.
threats.likelihood_justification: Disabling Claroty requires firewall management compromise (addressed in TM-IOT-007) or physical access to the SPAN port infrastructure. Semantic evasion requires deep knowledge of Claroty's detection models. Primarily a nation-state sophistication attack.
threats.impact_justification: Loss of OT IDS removes a critical detection capability but does not directly cause physical harm. Other controls (SIS, SCADA alarms, Splunk SIEM from other feeds) remain operational. Classified MEDIUM — degraded detection posture.
threats.iec62443_zone: DMZ — Conduit between OT and IT
mitigations: Deploy Claroty sensor heartbeat monitoring: if Claroty xDome stops reporting to Splunk SIEM for >60 seconds, auto-page SOC as potential IDS evasion event
Implement secondary passive tap on a different network segment (e.g., directly on the OT LAN switch between PLC and OPC-UA Gateway) providing independent visibility

Configure Claroty with both protocol anomaly detection AND behavioral baselining — detect semantically unusual commands (e.g., FC6 write to pressure setpoint register never before written to) even when protocol is valid
Protect SPAN port configuration with TACACS+ change management: firewall configuration changes require two-person approval logged in Splunk

threats.description: InfluxDB 2.7.5 stores all raw device telemetry (100M points/day) and serves as the primary data source for Grafana operational dashboards (C027). An attacker with a valid InfluxDB write token (obtainable via Vault compromise or direct exfiltration from a microservice pod environment variable) can write backdated, falsified data points into any measurement. Because InfluxDB's time-series model accepts writes for past timestamps, an attacker can retroactively alter historical sensor readings to conceal a process anomaly that already occurred — falsifying the forensic record. An attacker can also write values that will appear on Grafana dashboards, causing operators to make incorrect decisions based on fraudulent historical trend data. InfluxDB Flux queries used by Grafana panels do not cryptographically verify data integrity.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires valid write token. Vault dynamic secrets with 1h TTL limit the window, but a compromised Time-Series Ingestion Service (C016) pod exposes its token. InfluxDB 2.7.5 has known authentication vulnerabilities in some configurations.
threats.impact_justification: Falsified historical records corrupt post-incident forensics and can cause operators to incorrectly assess plant history during incident response. Grafana dashboard manipulation could suppress ongoing alarms. Classified HIGH due to forensic integrity impact and operator trust.
mitigations: Enable InfluxDB bucket-level write protection: configure separate read-only and write-only tokens; Grafana datasource uses read-only token
Implement InfluxDB write audit: use Telegraf plugin to generate SHA-256 checksums of each batch written, stored immutably in MinIO — enables post-hoc verification of data integrity
Configure InfluxDB retention policy to reject writes more than 5 minutes in the past (future timestamps already rejected) — prevents retroactive data falsification
Enable Vault dynamic secrets for InfluxDB write tokens (1h TTL as per architecture); alert in Splunk SIEM on any write token reuse outside expected service account pod lifecycle

Implement Grafana alerting on data freshness: if latest InfluxDB point for any critical sensor is older than 2 minutes during business hours, trigger NOC alert — detect write injection attacks that push old data

threats.description: Although the SIS is physically isolated via hardwired I/O and a unidirectional data diode, its status feed to the SCADA HMI (via OPC-UA read-only path) is visible on the supervisory network. An attacker who has compromised the SCADA Interface Layer (C006) can intercept the SIS OPC-UA status subscription and suppress the forwarding of SIS alarm states to the Historian (C007) and SCADA HMI (C005). Operators on the HMI will see 'SIS Normal' even if the SIS has activated an emergency shutdown. This suppression of SIS reporting creates a dangerous scenario where operators may attempt to manually override what appears to be a spurious SIS trip, restarting a process that the SIS correctly identified as unsafe. This TRITON/TRISIS attack pattern was deployed against Saudi Aramco in 2017.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires compromise of Level-2 SCADA Interface Layer — a high-effort attack requiring multiple prior steps. However, this is a known and documented ICS attack pattern (TRITON). The SIS isolation architecture specifically designed to prevent this, but the OPC-UA status path is a residual risk.
threats.impact_justification: Suppression of SIS alarm reporting could cause operators to manually override a legitimate emergency shutdown, restarting a hazardous process. CRITICAL PHYSICAL SAFETY IMPACT — potential for catastrophic physical harm, explosion, or toxic release. This is the most severe ICS attack scenario.
mitigations: Install physical alarm beacon (audible + visual, hardwired to SIS relay output) independent of SCADA HMI and OPC-UA path — SIS trip always physically visible in the control room regardless of software state
Implement an independent SIS status panel in the control room directly hardwired to SIS discrete output contacts — operators have a non-software path to SIS state
Enforce Waterfall Security unidirectional gateway (data diode) for SIS OPC-UA status feed — data physically cannot flow in the reverse direction, making SCADA-side suppression irrelevant
Train operators on 'trust the SIS' procedure: any discrepancy between SIS panel indicators and SCADA display must be treated as a SCADA anomaly, not a SIS fault
Implement IEC 61511 Functional Safety Management change control: any modification to SIS interface logic requires SIL verification by an independent functional safety assessor
Annual tabletop exercise: simulate SIS status suppression scenario; verify operator response procedures and escalation to process safety management

threats.description: Kafka Connect (C015) bridges MQTT device telemetry topics into Kafka raw-telemetry with Avro schema enforcement via Schema Registry. The Avro Schema Registry itself is an unauthenticated HTTP endpoint in many default deployments. An attacker who can reach the Schema Registry from within the Level-4 network can register a malicious evolved schema (backwards-compatible schema evolution allows adding fields with defaults) that includes a hidden field populated by Kafka Connect deserializer logic. More critically, if the Schema Registry is accessible without authentication, the attacker can modify the schema for the telemetry subject — changing field types, removing required fields, or injecting default values that override sensor readings. All subsequent telemetry messages that conform to the new schema would have their values silently altered by the Schema Registry's schema evolution logic before reaching InfluxDB.
threats.likelihood_justification: Confluent Schema Registry defaults to no authentication. In Kubernetes deployments, the Schema Registry service is often exposed cluster-wide without NetworkPolicy restrictions. Any pod in the cluster can reach the registry. Schema evolution tampering is a low-sophistication attack requiring only HTTP access.
threats.impact_justification: Schema tampering can alter data values across the entire telemetry pipeline, affecting both SCADA displays and ML model inputs. However, the attack is detectable via schema registry audit logs and data validation. Not a direct safety impact but corrupts the monitoring dataset.
mitigations: Enable Confluent Schema Registry authentication: SASL/PLAIN with per-application credentials; read-only credentials for consumers, write credentials only for Kafka Connect service account
Implement schema governance: all schema changes require change management ticket and automated Kafka Connect restart to prevent in-flight schema evolution abuse
Apply Cilium NetworkPolicy restricting Schema Registry access to only Kafka Connect and authorised consumer pods — no general cluster-wide HTTP access
Enable Schema Registry audit log: log all schema registration and modification events to Splunk SIEM — alert on schema changes outside business hours
Implement Avro value validation in Time-Series Ingestion Service (C016): validate sensor readings against engineering unit ranges (e.g., pressure must be 0-150 PSI) before writing to InfluxDB

threats.description: The Device Management Service (C018) is the authoritative registry for device certificates, firmware versions, and configuration hashes. It tracks expected-vs-actual configuration state. An attacker who compromises the Device Management Service API (via a Rust/Axum memory safety vulnerability or Kubernetes RBAC escalation to its service account) can register a rogue device identity in the registry — causing the Certificate Authority (C020) to issue a valid certificate for a non-existent or attacker-controlled device. This rogue device can then connect to all platform resources as a legitimate device, bypassing all subsequent certificate checks. The attacker can also mark a compromised edge device as 'patched to latest firmware' in the registry, masking the fact that the device is running attacker-supplied firmware and suppressing compliance alerts from the firmware version audit.
threats.likelihood_justification: Rust is memory-safe so exploit surface is primarily logic bugs in Axum route handlers and SQLx query construction. Kubernetes RBAC pivot (see TM-IOT-017) is more likely. Device Management Service is a high-value target for persistent access.
threats.impact_justification: Rogue device registration undermines the integrity of the entire device identity framework. Masking firmware version enables long-term undetected persistence of malicious firmware across the device fleet. High impact on platform trust integrity.
mitigations: Implement zero-trust device enrollment: new device registration in C018 requires a one-time enrollment token issued by an administrator via a separate out-of-band channel — no self-registration without prior authorization
Enable immutable audit log for all device registry mutations (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on devices table) using TimescaleDB append-only hypertable with write protection — forensic record cannot be altered
Implement firmware version attestation: edge devices must periodically report a TPM-signed quote of their current firmware measurement to C018 — tampered firmware produces a different measurement
Alert on any device status change (especially firmware_version field) that occurs outside a scheduled maintenance window — forward to Splunk SIEM
Apply Rust supply-chain security: pin all Cargo dependencies to specific versions with cargo-deny; scan Cargo.lock against OSV vulnerability database in CI/CD

threats.description: Grafana 10.3.3 is the only internet-exposed component in the Level-4 network (architecture marks C027 exposed=true). Grafana's datasource proxying feature allows any authenticated user to issue arbitrary queries to InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Loki, and Tempo via Grafana's backend proxy — effectively bypassing any database-level access controls. A Grafana user with 'Editor' role can create ad-hoc data source queries (Explore panel) that extract all 10 years of process historian data, all SCADA alarm history, and all security log data. Grafana's LDAP SAML SSO reduces credential theft risk, but Grafana has a history of privilege escalation CVEs allowing Viewer-to-Admin escalation (CVE-2021-43798 arbitrary file read; CVE-2022-39306 email validation bypass).
threats.likelihood_justification: Grafana is the only exposed Level-4 service and has had multiple critical CVEs. Public internet exposure makes it a priority target. A single Grafana account credential (LDAP or SAML session) provides access to all operational data via datasource proxy.
threats.impact_justification: Operational process data exfiltration from Grafana constitutes competitive intelligence theft and NERC-CIP-011 violation. Not a direct safety impact, but provides blueprint for planning a more targeted physical attack.
mitigations: Apply Grafana datasource permissions: restrict InfluxDB (operational data) datasource to Engineer and Admin roles only; Viewer role has read-only access to pre-built dashboards with no ad-hoc query capability
Update Grafana to latest patched version (10.4.x minimum); subscribe to Grafana Labs security advisories and apply patches within 48h of critical CVE publication
Enforce MFA on all Grafana accounts via SAML SSO MFA policy — LDAP password alone insufficient for login
Place Grafana behind a reverse proxy (nginx/HAProxy) with GeoIP blocking and rate limiting: restrict to corporate IP ranges or VPN-only access
Enable Grafana server-side rendering audit: log all Explore panel queries with user identity to Loki/Splunk — alert on bulk data exports (queries returning > 100,000 rows)
Implement network egress filtering on the Grafana pod: only permitted outbound connections to InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Loki, Tempo endpoints — prevent exfiltration via SSRF

threats.description: The Edge ML Inference service (C010) runs LSTM autoencoder and Isolation Forest models on the Jetson Orin NX to detect process anomalies at 100ms inference cycle. A model poisoning attack during the weekly SageMaker retraining cycle (see TM-IOT-016 for cloud-side data poisoning) results in a retrained model that has artificially raised anomaly thresholds — causing the edge inference service to classify actual process anomalies as 'normal'. The poisoned model is signed with the legitimate Ed25519 key (since the signing happens in SageMaker post-training, not post-review), passes the signature verification on the edge device, and is deployed silently. Additionally, TensorFlow Lite has known vulnerabilities (heap overflows in model parsing) — a maliciously crafted model file can exploit the TFLite runtime to achieve code execution on the Jetson.
threats.likelihood_justification: Model poisoning requires sustained access to cloud training data (see TM-IOT-016). TFLite RCE via malicious model is a high-sophistication attack requiring deep ML framework knowledge. Combined likelihood is low for most threat actors but feasible for nation-state.
threats.impact_justification: A poisoned anomaly detection model that misses real process deviations removes a critical layer of defence at the edge. Combined with MQTT telemetry manipulation, creates a condition where no automated system detects an attack. SAFETY-ADJACENT: delayed anomaly detection could delay human intervention.
mitigations: Implement model performance monitoring: compare edge model inference outputs against independent statistical threshold rules — if model anomaly score distribution shifts significantly from baseline, alert operations

Require human-in-the-loop approval for model deployment to edge fleet: SageMaker-trained models are staged in MinIO but not deployed until a data scientist reviews anomaly detection performance metrics on validation set
Validate TFLite model files against a schema before loading: reject models with unexpected layers, operations, or tensor shapes that deviate from the approved model architecture
Update TensorFlow Lite to latest patched version; subscribe to TensorFlow security advisories; apply patches via firmware update pipeline
Implement model version pinning: edge devices run a specific approved model version (recorded in C018 device registry); unauthorised model version changes trigger alert

threats.description: Splunk Enterprise Security 7.3.0 is the last line of defence for security event correlation and NERC-CIP compliance reporting. An attacker who has established a foothold in the Level-4 network can launch a log flood attack against the Splunk HEC endpoint (port 8088): generating millions of low-priority synthetic log events causes Splunk to throttle its indexing queue, delaying the ingestion of real security alerts by minutes or hours. During this delay window, the attacker can complete a process manipulation attack and have the evidence disappear into Splunk's indexing backlog — effectively neutralising the SIEM's detection capability at the critical moment. Additionally, disabling the Claroty-to-Splunk syslog path (by manipulating firewall rules or Claroty configuration) eliminates OT-specific detection while IT-side alerts continue flowing normally.
threats.likelihood_justification: Requires Level-4 network access and knowledge of Splunk HEC endpoint. HEC is protected by token authentication, but a compromised microservice with a Splunk HEC token can flood the index. Log flooding as a SIEM evasion technique is documented in MITRE ATT&CK T0872.
threats.impact_justification: SIEM flooding delays detection but does not directly cause harm. Other detection layers (Claroty IDS, SCADA alarms, PagerDuty direct escalation from Alert Processing Service) provide redundant paths. Classified MEDIUM — reduced detection fidelity.
mitigations: Implement Splunk HEC per-token rate limiting: each service HEC token has a maximum events/second quota; excess events are queued and a rate-limit alert is generated
Segregate Splunk HEC tokens by source: OT IDS alerts (Claroty) use a high-priority HEC token on a dedicated indexer; general microservice logs use a separate, rate-limited token
Enable Splunk Index Acknowledgment (HEC with ack=true): if Splunk stops returning acknowledgments, Alert Processing Service (C017) retains events in local Redis queue until SIEM recovers
Implement Splunk search monitoring: alert if OT IDS event count (sourcetype=claroty) drops below expected baseline for 10 consecutive minutes during business hours
Maintain a separate NERC-CIP compliance log archive in MinIO (C026) with WORM protection — Splunk outage does not affect compliance audit evidence

attack_scenarios.name: IT-to-OT Lateral Movement via Firewall Compromise
mitre_chain: T0869 - Standard Application Layer Protocol (initial phishing, C2 over HTTPS)
T0866 - Exploitation of Remote Services (PAN-OS CVE, TM-IOT-007)
T0886 - Remote Services (pivot to Level-2 via Firewall policy modification)
T0859 - Valid Accounts (OPC-UA anonymous session, TM-IOT-002)
T0831 - Manipulation of Control (setpoint manipulation via OPC-UA, TM-IOT-003)
attack_scenarios.description: Attacker compromises IT network via phishing, laterally moves to Palo Alto management interface using CVE-2024-3400, creates permissive OT/IT firewall rule, uses OPC-UA anonymous access on Kepware to modify PLC setpoints, causes controlled process deviation masked by SCADA alert suppression.
attack_scenarios.physical_outcome: Controlled process deviation causing overpressure or thermal event; SIS trip triggered; production downtime 8-72 hours; potential environmental release if SIS delayed
mitigation_gaps: OPC-UA anonymous auth not yet disabled on Kepware (TM-IOT-002 CRITICAL)
PAN-OS patch SLA not defined for CVSS 9.0+ (TM-IOT-007)
No independent SIS alarm panel separate from SCADA (TM-IOT-020)

attack_scenarios.name: OTA Firmware Supply Chain Implant
mitre_chain: T0862 - Supply Chain Compromise (MinIO or Vault compromise)
T0839 - Module Firmware (malicious firmware signed with stolen key)
T0856 - Spoof Reporting Message (firmware implant falsifies sensor telemetry)
T0815 - Denial of View (edge gateway suppresses anomaly alerts locally)
T0831 - Manipulation of Control (actuator commands injected via MQTT)
attack_scenarios.description: Attacker compromises the MinIO firmware bucket or Vault signing key, injects malicious firmware that reports false sensor values, blocks local anomaly detection, and awaits a trigger date to issue harmful actuator commands across the fleet.
attack_scenarios.physical_outcome: Coordinated multi-site physical attack across entire device fleet simultaneously. Potential for mass SIS trips, equipment damage, and supply disruption at national scale.
mitigation_gaps: Human-in-the-loop model deployment review not implemented (TM-IOT-024)
TPM-based key storage on edge devices not confirmed deployed (TM-IOT-008)

attack_scenarios.name: Telemetry Blindness + Process Sabotage Window
mitre_chain: T0814 - Denial of Service (Kafka cluster DoS, TM-IOT-006)
T0804 - Block Reporting Message (Claroty SPAN port disruption, TM-IOT-018)
T0878 - Alarm Suppression (SIS status suppression, TM-IOT-020)
T0855 - Unauthorized Command Message (Modbus write commands, TM-IOT-001)
attack_scenarios.estimated_attacker_sophistication: Advanced criminal / hacktivist with insider knowledge
attack_scenarios.description: Attacker executes a coordinated 'preparation and strike' sequence: disable Kafka (cloud visibility loss), disrupt Claroty SPAN (OT IDS loss), suppress SIS status display, then inject Modbus register writes to cause a process deviation during the 15-minute window where no monitoring system is operational.
attack_scenarios.physical_outcome: Process deviation (e.g., pressure vessel overpressure) during monitoring blackout. Delayed human detection and SIS response. Equipment damage and potential personnel injury.
mitigation_gaps: No Kafka circuit breaker independent of SIEM (TM-IOT-006)
Physical SIS alarm beacon not confirmed installed (TM-IOT-020)

immediate_action_items.action: Disable OPC-UA anonymous authentication on Kepware KEPServerEX immediately. Enforce SignAndEncrypt (Basic256Sha256) and reject all self-signed certificates. This is the highest-risk OT attack surface — a single command injection can cause physical process deviation.

Install physical SIS status indicator panel (hardwired to SIS relay contacts) in control room independent of SCADA HMI. This provides a non-software safety signal that cannot be suppressed by software attack (TRITON/TRISIS defence).
Apply PAN-OS patch for CVE-2024-3400 and restrict Palo Alto management interface to out-of-band management VLAN only. Define 24-hour SLA for CVSS 9.0+ firewall patches.

Enable Claroty xDome Modbus function code anomaly detection with baseline of expected FC codes and source IPs. Alert on FC6/FC16 write commands from non-HMI IP addresses. Plan migration to OPC-UA with certificate authentication.
Implement Kafka per-principal quotas and alert on consumer group lag > 60 seconds for raw-telemetry topic. Implement circuit breaker in Alert Processing Service for Kafka unavailability.

iec62443_compliance_gaps.requirement: IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1 (Human User Identification and Authentication)
iec62443_compliance_gaps.gap: OPC-UA anonymous access enabled on Kepware (TM-IOT-002)
Modbus TCP/IP has no message authentication or integrity protection (TM-IOT-001)
SCADA Interface Layer (C006) has no cryptographic audit trail for MQTT output (TM-IOT-010)
SIEM can be flooded suppressing audit accessibility during attacks (TM-IOT-025)
ML model deployment to edge lacks human review gate (TM-IOT-024)

nerc_cip_violations.violation: Potential for anonymous OPC-UA sessions to bypass electronic perimeter controls (TM-IOT-002)
Modbus TCP port 502 accessible without authentication (TM-IOT-001)
PI Historian bulk data export not rate-limited; potential for operational data exfiltration (TM-IOT-012)
Alert Processing Service repudiation gap means incident timeline may be unverifiable (TM-IOT-014)
